The examples so far were designed so the symmetry could be read off the
equations. A more realistic case is the classical enzyme-kinetics assay, where
the non-identifiabilities are genuinely not visible by inspection yet can still
be derived by hand and then confirmed by the method. A substrate is turned over
by an enzyme under the quasi-steady-state (Michaelis--Menten) law, and the
substrate is read out by some reporter of unknown gain $s$:
\begin{equation}
\dot S = -\,\frac{k_{\mathrm{cat}}\,E_{\mathrm{tot}}\,S}{K_m + S},
\qquad y = s\,S.
\label{eq:mm}
\end{equation}
The unknowns are the initial substrate $S_0$, the total enzyme
$E_{\mathrm{tot}}$, the Michaelis constant $K_m$, the readout gain $s$ and the
turnover number $k_{\mathrm{cat}}$, so $\dim z = 5$.

Two substitutions leave the entire observed trajectory $y(t)$, and therefore
every time derivative that the observability matrix collects, unchanged.

*The turnover--enzyme confounder.* The pair $(k_{\mathrm{cat}}, E_{\mathrm{tot}})$
enters \eqref{eq:mm} only through the product $V_{\max} = k_{\mathrm{cat}}\,
E_{\mathrm{tot}}$. Hence
\begin{equation}
k_{\mathrm{cat}} \to \lambda\,k_{\mathrm{cat}}, \qquad
E_{\mathrm{tot}} \to \lambda^{-1} E_{\mathrm{tot}}
\end{equation}
fixes $V_{\max}$, so $\dot S$, $S(t)$ and $y$ are untouched: only $V_{\max}$ is
identifiable, never the turnover number and the enzyme amount separately. The
generator is $k_{\mathrm{cat}}\partial_{k_{\mathrm{cat}}} -
E_{\mathrm{tot}}\partial_{E_{\mathrm{tot}}}$, the integer toric weights
$(k_{\mathrm{cat}}\!:1,\; E_{\mathrm{tot}}\!:-1)$.

*The readout-units confounder.* We never see $S$ in molar units, only $y = sS$.
The right-hand side of \eqref{eq:mm} is jointly homogeneous of degree one in the
amounts $(S, K_m, E_{\mathrm{tot}})$: replacing each by $\mu\cdot(\,\cdot\,)$
multiplies $\dot S$ by $\mu$, so $\tilde S = \mu S$ solves the rescaled system.
Compensating the gain by $s \to \mu^{-1}s$ keeps $y = sS$ fixed. The generator is
$S\partial_S + K_m\partial_{K_m} + E_{\mathrm{tot}}\partial_{E_{\mathrm{tot}}} -
s\,\partial_s$, the integer toric weights
$(S\!:1,\; E_{\mathrm{tot}}\!:1,\; K_m\!:1,\; s\!:-1)$.

Both substitutions are scalings, so the common invariants, the quantities the
data actually pin down, are $sS$ (the readout itself), $sK_m$ and
$s\,k_{\mathrm{cat}}E_{\mathrm{tot}} = sV_{\max}$: three functions, matching a
rank of three out of five. Both are scalings, so the `"observability"` engine
extracts them from the integer kernel and reports them as exact toric weights:

```{r mm, eval = haveSympy}
f <- eqnvec(S = "-kcat*Etot*S/(Km + S)")   # quasi-steady-state turnover
g <- eqnvec(y = "s*S")                      # readout of unknown gain s

res <- symmetryDetection(f, g, method = "observability", reconstruct = TRUE)
summary(res)
```

The engine returns two integer scalings spanning the same rank-$2$ lattice (each
carries `type = "scaling"`). The printed basis may combine the two hand-derived
confounders — a null space has no canonical basis — but the invariants it fixes
are exactly $sS$, $sK_m$ and $sV_{\max}$, so the verdict is the same. The
dedicated `"scaling"` engine returns the same lattice.

The verdict and the involved symbols come from modular rank computations at
generic points, not from a symbolic determining system, so `"observability"`
also applies to networks with dozens of states and conserved moieties. There the
closed-form reconstruction is attempted only for directions that couple few
unknowns; one that couples too many is returned with its support and
`reconstruct = FALSE`.

## Two rates on a curve

A non-identifiability need not be a straight ray through parameter space. The
plainest example is gene expression observed only at the protein level, an mRNA
$m$ transcribed at rate $k_{tx}$ and degraded at $d_m$, translated into protein
$p$ at rate $k_{tl}$ and degraded at $d_p$:
\begin{equation}
\dot m = k_{tx} - d_m\,m, \qquad
\dot p = k_{tl}\,m - d_p\,p, \qquad y = p.
\label{eq:gene}
\end{equation}
The mRNA is never measured. Then transcription and translation cannot be told
apart: more mRNA poorly translated and less mRNA well translated give the same
protein. Concretely, scaling the hidden mRNA by any $\lambda > 0$,
\begin{equation}
m \to \lambda\,m, \quad m_0 \to \lambda\,m_0, \quad
k_{tx} \to \lambda\,k_{tx}, \quad k_{tl} \to \lambda^{-1} k_{tl},
\label{eq:gene-sym}
\end{equation}
leaves $y = p$ invariant: the first equation becomes
$\dot{(\lambda m)} = \lambda k_{tx} - d_m(\lambda m)$, the transcription term
scaling with $m$, while the protein equation keeps $k_{tl} m = (\lambda^{-1}
k_{tl})(\lambda m)$ unchanged. The degradation rates $d_m, d_p$, set by the two
observed timescales, stay fixed.

Eliminating $\lambda$ from \eqref{eq:gene-sym}, the two rate constants are
confined to the level set
\begin{equation}
k_{tx}\,k_{tl} = \text{const},
\end{equation}
a hyperbola, not a line: any $(k_{tx}, k_{tl})$ on it produces exactly the same
protein trajectory (only their product, which sets the steady state
$p_\infty = k_{tx} k_{tl}/(d_m d_p)$, is identifiable). The orbit is curved, but
its generator is a plain scaling: the integer toric weights
$(k_{tx}\!:1,\; k_{tl}\!:-1,\; m\!:1)$, which is, up to its overall sign, the
single direction the method returns:

```{r gene, eval = haveSympy}
f <- eqnvec(m = "ktx - dm*m", p = "ktl*m - dp*p")   # mRNA hidden, protein seen
res <- symmetryDetection(f, eqnvec(y = "p"), method = "observability",
                         reconstruct = TRUE)
res$rank                                  # 5 of 6: one direction
res$symmetries[[1]]$generator
```

The opposite weights $k_{tx}\!:1$, $k_{tl}\!:-1$ trace the hyperbola
$k_{tx} k_{tl} = \text{const}$, and the $m\!:1$ weight records that the hidden
mRNA scales with $k_{tx}$ along it. Degradation rates and the protein do not
appear: the data fix them, and confine $k_{tx}, k_{tl}$ to the curve but no
further.

## EGF/EGFR feeding a MEK/ERK cascade

The directions so far were single scalings. A slightly larger but still
hand-checkable network shows how to *read* a whole set of directions at once, and
how each one turns back into an explicit transformation $\Phi_\varepsilon$ by the
integration of Section \ref{sec-flow}. Epidermal growth factor binds its receptor
to form an active complex that phosphorylates MEK, and phospho-MEK in turn
phosphorylates ERK; each kinase is returned by a constitutive phosphatase. Only
the two phospho-forms are measured, each on an unknown gain, so the ligand, the
receptor and the unphosphorylated kinase pools are hidden: the model is partially
observed. Every rate is mass-action, so every direction is rational and is
returned in closed form.

```{r egf-model, eval = haveSympy}
reactions <- eqnlist() |>
  addReaction("EGF + EGFR", "EGF_EGFR", "k_bind * EGF * EGFR")   |>
  addReaction("EGF_EGFR", "EGF + EGFR", "k_unbind * EGF_EGFR")   |>
  addReaction("MEK", "pMEK", "k_phos_MEK * EGF_EGFR * MEK")      |>
  addReaction("pMEK", "MEK", "k_dephos_MEK * pMEK")              |>
  addReaction("ERK", "pERK", "k_phos_ERK * pMEK * ERK")          |>
  addReaction("pERK", "ERK", "k_dephos_ERK * pERK")
reactions <- customTotals(reactions, list(
  totalEGF  = "EGF + EGF_EGFR", totalEGFR = "EGFR + EGF_EGFR",
  totalMEK  = "MEK + pMEK",     totalERK  = "ERK + pERK"))

observables <- eqnvec(pMEK_obs = "scale_pMEK * pMEK",
                      pERK_obs = "scale_pERK * pERK")
```

Running with `reduceCQ = FALSE` reports the directions in the species themselves
rather than through the moiety totals, which makes them easier to read:

```{r egf-run, eval = haveSympy}
egf <- symmetryDetection(reactions, observables, method = "observability",
                         reduceCQ = FALSE, reconstruct = TRUE)
summary(egf)
```

Rank $11$ of $15$: four directions, three scalings and one genuinely rational
(non-scaling) confounder. Each is forced by the partial observation. Every
direction is printed as its differential generator $\sum_i \xi_i\, \partial_{z_i}$
with its symmetry class (`scaling`, `polynomial`, ...) in brackets.

* **Receptor-module units**
  $(EGF\!:\!1,\, EGFR\!:\!1,\, EGF\_EGFR\!:\!1,\, k_{bind}\!:\!-1,\, k_{phosMEK}\!:\!-1)$.
  The ligand, the receptor and their complex are never seen directly, so their
  absolute concentration scale is free. Multiplying all three by $\lambda$ scales
  the bimolecular flux $k_{bind}\,EGF\,EGFR$ by $\lambda^2$, undone by
  $k_{bind}\!\to\!k_{bind}/\lambda$; and the activation of MEK,
  $k_{phosMEK}\,EGF\_EGFR\,MEK$, is held fixed by
  $k_{phosMEK}\!\to\!k_{phosMEK}/\lambda$, so the cascade downstream does not move.
* **MEK amount against its readout gain**
  $(MEK\!:\!1,\, pMEK\!:\!1,\, k_{phosERK}\!:\!-1,\, scale_{pMEK}\!:\!-1)$.
  The total MEK is unknown in absolute units: scaling MEK and pMEK by $\lambda$ is
  absorbed by the gain $scale_{pMEK}\!\to\!scale_{pMEK}/\lambda$ (the readout
  $scale_{pMEK}\,pMEK$ is fixed) and by $k_{phosERK}\!\to\!k_{phosERK}/\lambda$
  (the ERK activation $k_{phosERK}\,pMEK\,ERK$ is fixed).
* **ERK amount against its readout gain**
  $(ERK\!:\!1,\, pERK\!:\!1,\, scale_{pERK}\!:\!-1)$. The same calibration
  trade-off at the foot of the cascade; nothing lies downstream of ERK, so no
  rate compensates.
* **The binding equilibrium** (polynomial). Only $EGF$, $EGFR$ and $k_{unbind}$
  move, with the active complex $EGF\_EGFR$ and $k_{bind}$ held fixed. Because the
  cascade reads the receptor module only through that complex, the dissociation
  rate and the split between free ligand and free receptor cannot be told apart:
  they trade while the complex, and therefore everything observed, stays put. This
  is the one non-monomial direction, returned as the rational vector above.

**Reading a direction as a transformation.** A reported direction is the generator
$\xi$; the transformation it stands for is its flow $\Phi_\varepsilon$ from
Section \ref{sec-flow}. For a scaling the integration \eqref{eq:scaling-flow} is
immediate. The receptor-module direction integrates, with $\lambda = e^\varepsilon$,
to
\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
EGF &\mapsto \lambda\,EGF, &\quad
EGFR &\mapsto \lambda\,EGFR, &\quad
EGF\_EGFR &\mapsto \lambda\,EGF\_EGFR, \\
k_{bind} &\mapsto \lambda^{-1} k_{bind}, &\quad
k_{phosMEK} &\mapsto \lambda^{-1} k_{phosMEK},
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
every other coordinate fixed. Substituting into $k_{bind}\,EGF\,EGFR$ and
$k_{phosMEK}\,EGF\_EGFR\,MEK$ returns both fluxes to their values, so the output is
unchanged for every $\lambda > 0$: the reported integer weights are precisely this
power law.

The general direction is the same construction with a non-constant $\xi$. Writing
$C = EGF\_EGFR$ and $D = k_{bind}(EGF - EGFR)$, the flow \eqref{eq:flow} is
\begin{equation}
\frac{d\,EGF}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{-(EGF + C)}{D},\qquad
\frac{d\,EGFR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{EGFR + C}{D},\qquad
\frac{d\,k_{unbind}}{d\varepsilon} = 1,
\end{equation}
with $C$, $k_{bind}$ and everything downstream constant. It has no elementary
closed form, but integrating it explicitly is unnecessary to see what it does:
along the flow
\begin{equation}
\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}\big(k_{bind}\,EGF\,EGFR\big) = C,\qquad
\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}\big(k_{unbind}\,EGF\_EGFR\big) = C,
\end{equation}
so the binding and unbinding fluxes move in lockstep and their difference, the net
rate of complex formation, is preserved; $EGF\_EGFR$ itself is fixed
($\xi_{EGF\_EGFR} = 0$) while $k_{unbind}$ simply advances by $\varepsilon$. The
orbit re-partitions free ligand and receptor and shifts the dissociation rate
while holding the active complex and its dynamics, hence the entire measured
cascade, invariant: exactly the non-identifiability the support announced. This is
the general pattern. `symmetryDetection()` returns the generator $\xi$ exactly, and
the finite transformation $\Phi_\varepsilon$ follows by integrating it, in closed
form for a scaling and as a well-defined flow otherwise.

## A signalling network at scale

The same analysis runs on a model far larger than a symbolic determining system
can handle, and it exercises every ingredient at once: many conditions, event
gaps, a modularly solved steady state, and free Hill exponents. The example is a
two-receptor TGF$\beta$/SMAD signalling model with $26$ dynamic states and three
conserved SMAD moieties: receptor mRNA transcription under Hill-type
transcriptional feedback (a free exponent on each feedback species), translation
and degradation, ligand binding to each receptor and to the preformed receptor
complex, receptor internalisation, the active signalling complex, SMAD2 and SMAD3
phosphorylation modulated by feedback, the trimeric SMAD2--SMAD3--SMAD4 complex,
and three feedback genes (`FB2`, `FB3`, `FB4`). The reaction list is

```{r smad-model, eval = haveSympy}
addRC <- function(eq, from, to, rate, ...)
  addReaction(eq, from, to, rate, compartment = "Cell", ...)
addRE <- function(eq, from, to, rate, ...)
  addReaction(eq, from, to, rate, compartment = "extraCell", ...)

reactions <- eqnlist() |>
  addRC("", "bool_ActD", "0") |>
  addRC("", "bool_CHX", "0") |>
  addRC("", "bool_MG132", "0") |>
  addRE("", "TGFb", "0") |>
  addRC("", "R1mRNA",  "k_pr_R1mRNA * (1 + k_inh_R1mRNA_FB3 * FB3^nhill_R1) * (1 - bool_ActD)") |>
  addRC("", "R2mRNA",  "k_pr_R2mRNA / (1 + k_inh_R2mRNA_FB4 * FB4^nhill_R2) * (1 - bool_ActD)") |>
  addRC("", "FB2mRNA", "k_pr_FB2mRNA * C3^nhill_FB2mRNA / (Km_FB2mRNA^nhill_FB2mRNA + C3^nhill_FB2mRNA) * (1 - bool_ActD)") |>
  addRC("", "FB3mRNA", "k_pr_FB3mRNA * C3^nhill_FB3mRNA / (Km_FB3mRNA^nhill_FB3mRNA + C3^nhill_FB3mRNA) * (1 - bool_ActD)") |>
  addRC("", "FB4mRNA", "k_pr_FB4mRNA * C3^nhill_FB4mRNA / (Km_FB4mRNA^nhill_FB4mRNA + C3^nhill_FB4mRNA) * (1 - bool_ActD)") |>
  addRC("R1mRNA", "", "k_dg_R1mRNA * R1mRNA") |>
  addRC("R2mRNA", "", "k_dg_R2mRNA * R2mRNA") |>
  addRC("FB2mRNA", "", "k_dg_FB2 * FB2mRNA") |>
  addRC("FB3mRNA", "", "k_dg_FB3 * FB3mRNA") |>
  addRC("FB4mRNA", "", "k_dg_FB4 * FB4mRNA") |>
  addRC("", "R1", "k_pr_R1 * R1mRNA * (1 - bool_CHX)") |>
  addRC("", "R2", "k_pr_R2 * R2mRNA * (1 - bool_CHX)") |>
  addRC("", "FB2", "k_pr_FB2 * FB2mRNA * (1 - bool_CHX)") |>
  addRC("", "FB3", "k_pr_FB3 * FB3mRNA * (1 - bool_CHX)") |>
  addRC("", "FB4", "k_pr_FB4 * FB4mRNA * (1 - bool_CHX)") |>
  addRC("R1", "", "k_dg_R1 * R1 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("R2", "", "k_dg_R2 * R2 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("FB2", "", "k_dg_FB2 * FB2 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("FB3", "", "k_dg_FB3 * FB3 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("FB4", "", "k_dg_FB4 * FB4 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("R1 + R2", "R1_R2", "k_act_R1_R2 * R1 * R2") |>
  addRC("R1_R2", "R1 + R2", "k_deact_R1_R2 * R1_R2") |>
  addRC("R1_R2", "", "k_dg_R1_R2 * R1_R2 * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("R2 + TGFb", "R2_TGFb",
        "k_act_R2_TGFb * TGFb * R2 / (km_R2 + R2 + TGFb)",
        rateCompartment = "Cell") |>
  addRC("R2_TGFb", "R2 + TGFb", "k_deact_R2_TGFb * R2_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R2_TGFb", "R2_TGFb_int", "k_int_R2_TGFb * R2_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R2_TGFb_int", "TGFb", "k_decay_R2_TGFb_int * R2_TGFb_int") |>
  addRC("R2_TGFb_int", "", "k_dg_R2_TGFb_int * R2_TGFb_int * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("R1 + TGFb", "R1_TGFb",
        "k_act_R1_TGFb * TGFb * R1 / (km_R1 + R1 + TGFb)",
        rateCompartment = "Cell") |>
  addRC("R1_TGFb", "R1 + TGFb", "k_deact_R1_TGFb * R1_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R1_TGFb", "R1_TGFb_int", "k_int_R1_TGFb * R1_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R1_TGFb_int", "TGFb", "k_decay_R1_TGFb_int * R1_TGFb_int") |>
  addRC("R1_TGFb_int", "", "k_dg_R1_TGFb_int * R1_TGFb_int * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("R1 + R2_TGFb", "R1_R2_TGFb", "k_act_R1_R2_TGFb * R1 * R2_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R2 + R1_TGFb", "R1_R2_TGFb", "k_act_R2_R1_TGFb * R2 * R1_TGFb") |>
  addRC("R1_R2 + TGFb", "R1_R2_TGFb",
        "k_act_R1_R2_TGFb_direct * TGFb * R1_R2 / (km_R1_R2 + R1_R2 + TGFb)",
        rateCompartment = "Cell") |>
  addRC("R1_R2_TGFb", "",
        "(k_dg_R1_R2_TGFb + k_dg_R1_R2_TGFb_FB1 * FB2) * R1_R2_TGFb * (1 - bool_MG132)") |>
  addRC("Smad2", "pSmad2",
        "(k_phospho_pS2 / (1 + k_inh_pSmad2_FB2 * FB2)) * Smad2 * R1_R2") |>
  addRC("Smad2", "pSmad2",
        "(k_phospho_pS2 / (1 + k_inh_pSmad2_FB1 * FB2)) * Smad2 * R1_R2_TGFb") |>
  addRC("pSmad2", "Smad2", "k_dephos_S2 * pSmad2") |>
  addRC("Smad3", "pSmad3",
        "(k_phospho_pS3 / (1 + k_inh_pSmad3_FB2 * FB2)) * Smad3 * R1_R2") |>
  addRC("Smad3", "pSmad3",
        "(k_phospho_pS3 / (1 + k_inh_pSmad3_FB2 * FB2)) * Smad3 * R1_R2_TGFb") |>
  addRC("pSmad3", "Smad3", "k_dephos_S3 * pSmad3") |>
  addRC("pSmad2 + pSmad3 + Smad4", "C3",
        "k_form_S4Coip * pSmad2 * pSmad3 * Smad4") |>
  addRC("C3", "Smad2 + pSmad3 + Smad4", "k_dissolve_C3_dp2 * C3") |>
  addRC("C3", "pSmad2 + Smad3 + Smad4", "k_dissolve_C3_dp3 * C3")

reactions$compartments$Cell$volume      <- "1"
reactions$compartments$extraCell$volume <- "volumeEC"
reactions <- customTotals(reactions, list(totalSMAD2 = "Smad2 + pSmad2 + C3",
                                          totalSMAD3 = "Smad3 + pSmad3 + C3",
                                          totalSMAD4 = "Smad4 + C3"))
```

The readouts are the two receptor levels, the phospho- and total-SMAD levels, the
SMAD4 co-immunoprecipitation, the two receptor mRNAs and the ligand. In the
experiment they are measured on $\log_{10}$ scale, which has the same level sets as
$\mathrm{scale}\cdot h$, so they are supplied in that rational form. The experiment
toggles transcription (actinomycin D), translation (cycloheximide) and the
proteasome (MG132), or knocks a receptor down; each perturbation is one condition.

```{r smad-setup, eval = haveSympy}
observables <- eqnvec(
  R1_obs          = "scale_R1 * R1",
  R2_obs          = "scale_R2 * R2",
  pSmad2_obs      = "scale_pSmad2 * (pSmad2 + C3)",
  pSmad3_obs      = "scale_pSmad3 * (pSmad3 + C3)",
  TSmad2_obs      = "scale_TSmad2 * (Smad2 + pSmad2 + C3)",
  TSmad3_obs      = "scale_TSmad3 * (Smad3 + pSmad3 + C3)",
  Smad4_CoIP_obs  = "scale_CoIP * C3",
  TGFBR1_mRNA_obs = "scale_R1mRNA * R1mRNA",
  TGFBR2_mRNA_obs = "scale_R2mRNA * R2mRNA",
  TGFb_obs        = "scale_TGFb * TGFb")

events <- eventlist() |>
  addEvent(var = "TGFb",       time = 0,   value = "init_TGFb",      method = "replace") |>
  addEvent(var = "bool_CHX",   time = -30, value = "var_bool_CHX",   method = "replace") |>
  addEvent(var = "bool_MG132", time = -30, value = "var_bool_MG132", method = "replace") |>
  addEvent(var = "bool_ActD",  time = -30, value = "var_bool_ActD",  method = "replace")

# one condition per perturbation; each row bakes the switch values and the TGFb
# dose, and the receptor knockdowns rename a synthesis rate
cond.grid <- data.frame(
  Pertubation = c("Ctrl", "ActD", "CHX", "MG132", "R1Knd", "R2Knd"),
  init_TGFb = 1, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
cond.grid$var_bool_ActD  <- ifelse(cond.grid$Pertubation == "ActD",  1, 0)
cond.grid$var_bool_CHX   <- ifelse(cond.grid$Pertubation == "CHX",   1, 0)
cond.grid$var_bool_MG132 <- ifelse(cond.grid$Pertubation == "MG132", 1, 0)
cond.grid$k_pr_R1mRNA <- ifelse(cond.grid$Pertubation == "R1Knd",
                                "k_pr_R1mRNA_R1Knd", "k_pr_R1mRNA")
cond.grid$k_pr_R2mRNA <- ifelse(cond.grid$Pertubation == "R2Knd",
                                "k_pr_R2mRNA_R2Knd", "k_pr_R2mRNA")
rownames(cond.grid) <- cond.grid$Pertubation
cond.grid$Pertubation <- NULL

# broadcast an identity trafo over the grid and insert each row's columns, giving
# one eqnvec per condition (a numeric bake for the switches/dose, a rename per knockdown)
cond.trafo <- eqnvec() |>
  define("x~x", x = getParameters(reactions, events)) |>
  branch(table = cond.grid, apply = "insert")
```

The boolean switches and the ligand are the externally driven inputs, declared
with `forcings`. The conditions enter as a per-condition `trafo` list, built by
broadcasting an identity map over the grid and inserting each row's covariates
with `branch()`; this is the general route when a perturbation renames a rate
(the receptor knockdowns) as well as fixing a switch. `equilibrate = TRUE` imposes
the pre-stimulus resting state with all forcings at zero and no events, solved
exactly over a finite field; the receptor-ligand complexes then fall out as zero
on their own, so nothing needs to be declared `fixed`, and the free Hill exponents
are recast to rational coordinates (Section \ref{sec-hill}) so the finite-field
solve still applies. The TGF$\beta$ dose at $t = 0$ is applied as an event on top
of that resting state, and the switch events at $t = -30$ open a second analytic
segment propagated exactly across the gap (Section \ref{sec-segments}); the
analysis starts at the earliest event time on its own. `cores` runs the
steady-state solves of the stacked conditions in parallel.

```{r smad-run, eval = haveSympy, warning = FALSE}
smad <- symmetryDetection(
  reactions, observables, method = "observability",
  events = events, trafo = cond.trafo,
  forcings = c("bool_ActD", "bool_CHX", "bool_MG132", "TGFb"),
  equilibrate = TRUE, reduceCQ = TRUE, reconstruct = TRUE, cores = 4)

summary(smad)
```

Six conditions, each split into two analytic segments by the pre-stimulus switch
events, are stacked, and the analysis reaches rank $65$ of $74$, leaving nine
non-identifiable directions. All nine are scalings, obtained exactly from the
stacked integer kernel, so each carries its integer weights, printed above; the
symbols in each weight are the factors it couples. Every free Hill exponent is
identifiable -- two survive only inside a weight. The nine fall into five groups,
each a product the data fix while its factors stay free.

* **Receptor mRNA synthesis against its readout gain** (two directions). For each
  receptor the mRNA synthesis rates (`k_pr_R1mRNA` and its knockdown variant
  `k_pr_R1mRNA_R1Knd`) and the protein synthesis rate `k_pr_R1` trade against the
  mRNA readout gain `scale_R1mRNA`; symmetrically for receptor 2.
* **Feedback protein against feedback mRNA** (three directions). Each feedback
  gene's protein synthesis `k_pr_FB*` trades against its mRNA synthesis
  `k_pr_FB*mRNA`, the unobserved intermediate mRNA absorbing the difference.
* **Linear feedback strength against feedback abundance** (one direction). `FB2`
  enters the phosphorylation and complex-degradation terms *linearly*
  (`k_inh_pSmad2_FB1`, `k_inh_pSmad2_FB2`, `k_inh_pSmad3_FB2`,
  `k_dg_R1_R2_TGFb_FB1`), so those action strengths confound with FB2's synthesis
  `k_pr_FB2` as an ordinary integer scaling.
* **Hill feedback strength, exponent-weighted** (two directions). The receptor
  mRNA feedbacks enter through `k_inh_R1mRNA_FB3 * FB3^nhill_R1`; the inhibition
  strength trades against the feedback synthesis `k_pr_FB3` with the free Hill
  exponent in the weight, $\xi_{k_{inh}} = -nhill_{R1}\,k_{inh}$ (and the same for
  receptor 2). The exponents `nhill_R1`, `nhill_R2` are themselves identifiable.
* **SMAD pool against the readout gains** (one direction). The conserved SMAD
  totals and the feedback Michaelis constants `Km_FB*mRNA` trade against the SMAD
  readout gains (`scale_pSmad2/3`, `scale_TSmad2/3`, `scale_CoIP`) and the
  complex-formation rate `k_form_S4Coip` (with weight $2$).

The boolean switches and the known TGF$\beta$ dose are constants of each condition
and appear in none of the directions. Each group is removed by the
reparametrisation shown earlier: fix a feedback gene's protein synthesis so the
mRNA synthesis absorbs it, provide an absolute receptor-mRNA measurement (or fix
`scale_R1mRNA`, `scale_R2mRNA`), and fix the readout gains against an absolute SMAD
calibration.


# Scope and limitations {#sec-scope}

* *Structural, not practical.* The methods use only $f$ and $g$. They
  cannot detect practical non-identifiability, which depends on the data
  and the noise.
* *Local rank versus explicit transformation.* `"observability"`
  decides local identifiability and names the unconstrained unknowns at
  generic points. `"polynomial"` returns the explicit finite transformation
  when it exists within the polynomial ansatz, at higher cost that grows
  with `pMax` and the number of variables.
* *Admissible observables.* `"polynomial"` requires a rational gradient of
  $g$, which includes rational maps and logarithms of rational maps.
  `"observability"` requires $f$ and $g$ themselves to be rational, since
  its finite-field construction is built from the field operations only; a
  logarithmic readout is supplied in the equivalent rational form
  $\mathrm{scale}\cdot h$ (Section \ref{sec-finitefield}), and a non-rational gradient
  (e.g. $\exp$, $\sqrt{\ }$) is outside the scope of both methods.
* *Conserved quantities.* With an `eqnlist` input and `reduceCQ = TRUE`,
  conserved moieties are eliminated before the search, which reduces the
  number of variables.
* *Reproducibility.* The observability point is chosen deterministically,
  so repeated runs give the same verdict; a custom evaluation point can be
  supplied through `point`.

# References

Ben-Or, M., and Tiwari, P. (1988). A deterministic algorithm for sparse
multivariate polynomial interpolation. In *Proceedings of the 20th Annual ACM
Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC)*, 301--309.

Hong, H., Ovchinnikov, A., Pogudin, G., and Yap, C. (2020). Global
identifiability of differential models. *Communications on Pure and Applied
Mathematics* 73, 1831--1879.

Massonis, G., and Villaverde, A. F. (2020). Finding and breaking Lie symmetries:
implications for structural identifiability and observability in biological
modelling. *Symmetry* 12, 469.

Merkt, B., Timmer, J., and Kaschek, D. (2015). Higher-order Lie symmetries
in identifiability and predictability analysis of dynamic models.
*Physical Review E* 92, 012920.

Pohjanpalo, H. (1978). System identifiability based on the power series
expansion of the solution. *Mathematical Biosciences* 41, 21--33.

Saccomani, M. P., Audoly, S., and D'Angiò, L. (2003). Parameter identifiability
of nonlinear systems: the role of initial conditions. *Automatica* 39, 619--632.

Savageau, M. A., and Voit, E. O. (1987). Recasting nonlinear differential
equations as S-systems: a canonical nonlinear form. *Mathematical Biosciences*
87, 83--115.

Sedoglavic, A. (2002). A probabilistic algorithm to test local algebraic
observability in polynomial time. *Journal of Symbolic Computation* 33,
735--755.

Villaverde, A. F., Barreiro, A., and Papachristodoulou, A. (2016).
Structural identifiability of dynamic systems biology models. *PLoS
Computational Biology* 12, e1005153.
